title: Agricultural water security under water scarcity: structural pat-terns, systemic blind spots, and research frontiers in semi-arid regions: A systematic review
abstract: Water scarcity is intensifying in rainfed and semi-arid agricultural regions, where increasing water demand and climate variability threaten food production and rural livelihoods. In this context, decentralized water storage systems, particularly Rainwater Harvesting (RWH), have emerged as key strategies to increase water security in agriculture. However, the current scientific literature remains fragmented, sometimes prioritizing hydrological performance and underrepresenting the agricultural, socioeconomic, and long-term dynamics of the system. This study presents a systematic literature review to critically assess the role of RWH systems in agricultural water security. The review follows the PRISMA 2020 protocol to ensure transparency and reproducibility and applies the Methodi Ordinatio to classify and select high-impact studies. A total of 22 peer-reviewed articles from the Scopus, ScienceDirect, and SciELO databases were analyzed.

title: Semantic Representation of Mental Accounts Under Financial Scarcity: How do we think about spending?
abstract: Financial scarcity has been shown to impact cognition, influencing how individuals allocate attention, evaluate trade-offs, and make economic decisions. Within behavioural economics, mental accounting theory suggests that people cognitively categorize financial resources into separate “accounts” that guide spending behaviour. However, the underlying cognitive structure of these mental accounts under conditions of financial scarcity remains largely unexplored. To address this gap, the present study applies semantic network methods from network science to model how expenditure-related concepts are organized in memory, enabling a systematic analysis of mental account representations at the network level. Specifically, we examine whether the semantic organization of expenditure concepts differs across socioeconomic groups (financially constrained vs financially secure), and whether financial scarcity is associated with more rigid and compartmentalized mental account structures. By identifying how scarcity shapes the cognitive organization of financial concepts, this research aims to advance understanding of how economic constraints become embedded in cognition and inform interventions designed to improve financial decision-making.

title: How Do Intuition and Reflection Affect Cooperation Under Resource Scarcity Threat in the US?
abstract: This experiment examines the effects of thinking styles and resource scarcity threat on cooperation in a one-shot Public Goods Game (PGG) in a US sample.

title: How Do Intuition and Reflection Affect Cooperation Under Resource Scarcity Threat?
abstract: In this experiment, we will examine the effects of intuitive and reflective thinking styles and heightened resource scarcity threat perceptions on cooperation in a one-shot Public Goods Game (PGG) in a Turkish sample.

title: A Replication of “The Moderating Role of Resource Scarcity on the Effect of Reflective and Intuitive Thinking on Cooperation”
abstract: This study will conduct a high-powered replication of a previous study in which we examined the impacts of both reflective and intuitive thinking on cooperation, while also investigating whether these effects vary based on subjective socioeconomic status (SES) on a US sample preselected based on low and high subjective SES on Prolific.

title: The Moderating Role of Resource Scarcity on the Effect of Reflective and Intuitive Thinking on Cooperation
abstract: In this study, we will examine the impacts of both reflective and intuitive thinking on cooperation in a non-WEIRD sample (Türkiye), while also investigating whether these effects vary based on income status.

title: Available for a limited time only: How a scarcity Miranda administration tactic influences judges’, suspects’, and jurors’ perceptions of coercion. (Exp. 1)
abstract: The current experiment focuses on the effect of a scarcity Miranda administration tactic on mock suspects’ Miranda waiver likelihood, perceptions of coercion, and cost-benefit analysis.

title: Money vs. time scarcity effect on faulty products_Social Includion moderation
abstract: In this project, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affect consumers' decisions related to faulty products and how feeling socially included can moderate the effect.

title: From Scarcity to Abundance: A Scoping Review of Artificial Intelligence Generated Synthetic Data for Enhancing Clinical Diagnostic Skills
abstract: The advent of multimodal generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) offers novel educational paradigms in ophthalmology and optometry. Synthetic data, ranging from AI-generated clinical vignettes (via Large Language Models) to high-fidelity synthetic ocular images (via Generative Adversarial Networks or diffusion models) and AI-generated surgical videos, has the potential to standardize training. However, the extent, nature, and educational focus of the literature evaluating these multimodal tools for early-career professionals remain unclear. This scoping review aims to map the existing evidence, identify how these modalities are being utilized, and highlight gaps in current educational research.

title: Agricultural water security under water scarcity: structural pat-terns, systemic blind spots, and research frontiers in semi-arid regions: A systematic review
abstract: This record documents the protocol and workflow for selecting studies (PRISMA) for the systematic review on agricultural water security in semi-arid regions. Data were collected from the ScienceDirect and SciELO databases, resulting in the final analysis of 74 articles.

title: SCARcE: The effect of scarcity and social comparison on fear acquisition and extinction
abstract: Common mental disorders, including anxiety disorders, are highly prevalent. However, in contrast to popular belief, anxiety disorders are not a disease of affluence. All around the world, anxiety disorders are more prevalent in people who live in poverty. In wealthy nations, however, literature suggests that the effects of income inequality may be a stronger determinant of well-being than absolute income. This sparked a debate on whether economic growth or lower inequality are critical for societal flourishing and general mental well-being. Nonetheless, to date, no experimental designs looked simultaneously at absolute scarcity and social comparison to assess their relative importance in the development of mental problems. As a consequence, our study will examine whether the presence of social comparison influences fear conditioning processes (i.e., fear acquisition and fear extinction, two crucial processes for the onset and perpetuation of anxiety disorders) even in the absence of absolute scarcity. Demonstrating that social comparison has an effect beyond absolute scarcity would reveal that inequality can impact well-being even among those not in absolute poverty. Moreover, investigating the combined effects of absolute scarcity and social comparison upon fear conditioning processes will likely lead to important first insights into how these concepts might jointly influence mental health in those living in poverty. The implications of this study reach further than just psychology and can help bring a new perspective to sociological and economic theory.

title: Blaming the Self or the System: How Stigma Shapes the Psychological Impact of Scarcity
abstract:
Common mental disorders, including anxiety disorders, are highly prevalent. However, in contrast to popular belief, anxiety disorders are not a disease of affluence. In all countries around the world, anxiety disorders are more prevalent in people who live in poverty. Nonetheless, psychological research on anxiety disorders and sociological research on poverty have largely been conducted separately, thereby neglecting the potential interaction between the two. As a consequence, the mechanism(s) underlying this relationship between poverty and anxiety disorders are unclear. People living in poverty are constantly faced with a sense of scarcity: they have too few resources to obtain a proper living standard. As a consequence, people in poverty struggle to survive within their environment, and the increased risk for anxiety disorders and accompanying (work) disability further intensifies this struggle. In order to uncover how to break this vicious cycle, we need to uncover which mechanism(s) play a role in the heightened risk of anxiety disorders in those experiencing scarcity.

Recent work highlights that the social meaning attached to poverty may play a strong role in its psychological consequences. Individuals who internalize stigma (perceiving poverty as a personal failure rather than a structural problem), report higher levels of shame, stress and reduced self-efficacy. On the other hand attributing poverty to systemic or societal factors may buffer these effects by reducing self-blame and perceived controllability. Understanding how such different forms of stigma shape the experience of scarcity is essential, as stigma not only influences emotional responses but can also affect learning, motivation, and avoidance behaviors that maintain anxiety over time.

In the current study, we will focus on the influence of experimentally induced scarcity on learning mechanisms that play a crucial role in the onset and perpetuation of anxiety disorders; more specifically extinction. Besides that we will also focus on the role of stigma around poverty and what kind of influence different types of stigma (empasis on yourself or emphasis on society) might have on the experience of scarcity.

title: Study on the Perception of Scarcity and Interpersonal Objectification: An Analysis of Migrant Individuals
abstract:
In recent decades, the issue of economic scarcity has attracted growing interest in both social and behavioral psychology and economics. This interest concerns not only the objective condition of insufficient material resources, but also—and above all—the subjective perception of living in a state of lack relative to one’s needs. Such a perception, regardless of actual economic availability, profoundly affects cognitive and decision-making processes: it reduces the ability to sustain attention, promotes a short-term–oriented perspective, and hinders the planning of longer-term strategies (Shafir & Mullainathan, 2013). At the psychological level, scarcity translates into experiences of stress, vulnerability, and exclusion, with repercussions for both individual well-being and collective dynamics, as it shapes the way people interpret interpersonal relationships and power relations.

A closely related and crucial issue is the phenomenon of objectification. Objectification occurs when an individual is reduced to a means, a function, or an object, rather than being recognized as a person endowed with subjectivity and dignity. Philosophical and psychological scholarship (Nussbaum, 1995; Volpato, 2019) has shown that objectification emerges particularly strongly in contexts marked by structural inequalities, where resource scarcity tends to heighten the tendency to instrumentalize others. In social psychology, this process is linked to phenomena of dehumanization and stereotyping, which strip individuals of their uniqueness by assimilating them into stigmatized social categories. Moreover, objectification is not limited to the interpersonal dimension: individuals living in conditions of economic disadvantage may internalize devaluing representations, leading to a reduced sense of agency and fostering vicious cycles of exclusion and marginalization.
At the same time, recent perspectives suggest that scarcity does not necessarily lead to uniformly negative interpersonal outcomes, as under certain conditions it may also be associated with increased sensitivity to others’ needs and with prosocial orientations, such as the tendency to provide help, particularly in contexts of shared vulnerability.

In contrast to these dynamics, the literature recognizes the protective role of social support. This term refers to the set of resources derived from meaningful relationships, which may provide emotional support (empathy, closeness, listening), informational support (guidance, advice), or material support (concrete assistance). What matters is not only the actual availability of these resources, but above all the perception of being able to rely on others (Cohen & Wills, 1985). This perception is consistently associated with lower levels of stress, greater resilience, and better psychological well-being outcomes. Well-established studies (Zimet et al., 1988; Lakey & Cohen, 2000) indicate that social support performs a buffering function, that is, it mitigates the impact of stressful events by strengthening individuals’ internal resources. In situations of economic scarcity, the presence of a reliable relational network can reduce feelings of isolation and mistrust, counteracting the tendency to experience oneself or others in objectifying terms and potentially fostering prosocial responses toward others. In this sense, perceived support acts as a psychological and social resource capable of shaping the interpersonal consequences of scarcity. With regard to the target population of the present research, this experience may be particularly relevant, as it concerns migrants.

Taken together, these elements suggest that the joint analysis of economic scarcity, objectification, prosocial tendencies, and social support offers a valuable framework for understanding the mechanisms of scarcity and their interpersonal implications. These dynamics may be mediated or moderated by social support or, conversely, may fuel experiences of exclusion, particularly in contexts characterized by growing inequalities. The present project is situated within this perspective and aims to further explore the interrelations among these constructs.

The decision to focus the investigation on the migrant population stems from the fact that individuals undergoing a recent migratory experience are often exposed to conditions of economic, social, and relational vulnerability. In such contexts, the perception of scarcity may be more pronounced and intertwined with dynamics of objectification and discrimination that reduce recognition of the individual’s full subjectivity. At the same time, social support plays a crucial role, as networks of formal and informal ties—linked to either the place of origin or the host society—can constitute a protective factor capable of fostering resilience, inclusion, well-being, and prosocial engagement. Investigating these constructs within the migrant population thus makes it possible to shed light on particularly significant psychological and social mechanisms, with implications both at the theoretical level and in terms of possible applications aimed at inclusive practices and community-based interventions.

title: The relationship between financial scarcity and cooperation：A meta-analysis
abstract: We plan to reveal the overall relationship between financial scarcity and cooperation and to explore the potential reasons for the different results by examining theoretical and methodological factors.

title: Not Enough to Manage It All: Resource Scarcity and Self-Regulation Across Multiple Goals – A Laboratory Study
abstract: Resource scarcity can fundamentally alter how individuals approach their goals. In this laboratory study, we experimentally manipulate perceived scarcity to examine its impact on goal-setting behaviors, expected outcomes, and perceived conflict across multiple life domains.

title: Money vs. time scarcity effect on faulty products and the moderation of social inclusion
abstract: In this project, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affect consumers' decisions related to faulty products and how feeling socially included can moderate the effect.

title: Money vs. time scarcity effect on faulty products and the moderation of social inclusion
abstract: In this project, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affect consumers' decisions related to faulty products and how feeling socially included can moderate the effect.

title: THE COLLAPSE OF NOISE: Retrieval Scarcity and the Structural Reordering of the Internet
abstract: This paper introduces retrieval scarcity as a new infrastructural condition emerging from the rising cost of computation and the end of the abundance‑based internet. It argues that as AI inference becomes expensive and platforms reduce indexing capacity, low‑structure, high‑volume content—“noise”—collapses, while structured, coherent, machine‑legible bodies of work gain visibility and durability. The essay situates retrieval scarcity within the broader SignalRupture canon, including Systemic Erosion, Infrastructural Exposure, and Quiet Governance, and demonstrates how platform behavior is shifting from engagement optimization to retrieval triage. It formalizes the role of SignalRupture as an infrastructural method for producing durable knowledge under conditions of systemic instability, and outlines the implications of retrieval scarcity for creators, platforms, and AI systems.

title: Money vs. time scarcity effect on faulty products
abstract: In this project, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affect consumers' decisions related to faulty products.

title: Money vs. time scarcity effect on faulty products
abstract: In this project, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affect consumers' decisions related to faulty products.

title: Scarcity Project
abstract: This project investigates the role of scarcity mindset of the experience of insufficient resources on consumer preference for algorithm versus human, as well as the underlying psychological mechanism driving the effect.

title: Money vs. time scarcity effect on faulty products
abstract: In this project, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affect consumers' decisions related to faulty products.

title: Does perceived scarcity affect third-party punishment and compensation preference?
abstract:
The aim of this study is to investigate the effects of perceived financial scarcity on third-party 
punishment and compensation (TPPC) decisions. 
Recent evidence highlights the interplay between top-down (intrinsic, goal-directed) and bottom-up  (exogenous, driven by the environment) cognitive processing in TPPC decisions. In a recent study (Civai et al, 2025), we used a TPPC game whereby participants played multiple rounds as observers of a hypothetical exchange between two other players, A and B, who changed identity in each round. Each player (A, B and the participant) started each round with 200 chips (monetary units); however, player A was always the “offender”, and in every round would steal some chips from a powerless “victim”, player B. The participant would have to decide whether they wanted to spend some of their 200 chips to punish A, or to compensate B. Our results suggest that individuals exhibit an intrinsic top-down bias toward focusing on the offender, and punishing; however, when forced (perceptually) to focus on the victim, by making the victim's payoff the most salient information, this bias disappears, suggesting that these decisions are also influenced by exogenous, bottom up factors, particularly the available information.
Scarcity, whether real or perceived, imposes significant cognitive constraints, narrowing individuals’ cognitive bandwidth and shifting their focus toward immediate, pressing needs. This cognitive load diminishes performance and impedes overall cognitive capacity (Zhao & Tomm, 2018), frequently resulting in myopia, impulsivity, and an increased reliance on decision-making heuristics. These effects of scarcity on cognition suggest individuals may rely less on resource-intensive, top-down processing and more heavily on automatic, bottom-up processing. 
The existing literature reveals considerable heterogeneity and context-specificity in how scarcity impacts prosocial behaviours. For instance, financial scarcity often reduces prosocial behaviours, while contexts involving resource conservation (e.g., water scarcity) can heighten moral obligations and prosocial conduct (Civai et al, 2024). However, the influence of scarcity on third-party moral decision-making remains underexplored.
Building on our previous work on TPPC, this study aims to explore how perceived financial scarcity modulates preferences between punishment and compensation in third-party moral contexts. 
Specifically, we will investigate whether scarcity amplifies attentional biases (bottom-up) or fosters greater dependence on heuristic processing (top-down) when individuals make socio-moral decisions as impartial observers, or both.

title: Scarcity and debt managment (Study 3)
abstract: The recent economic crisis has highlighted the need to study the effect of economic and financial constraints on financial choice, particularly those involved in credit lines. Literature shows that people experiencing financial constraints are more willing to indebt, therefore the goal of the project is to investigate the effect of perceived scarcity in the choice of loan repayment modality under conditions of difficulty with installment payments. We will also consider the potential mediators involved. The study will investigate the mediating effect of perceived risk associated with lack of resources and the psychological ownership of borrowed money (POBM) on loan repayment mode (refinance vs. renegotiate).

title: The effect of different types of money scarcity on financial services
abstract: In this study, we investigate how financial constraints due to limited money or financial constraints due to social comparisons will affect consumers' preference toward products related to financial advice that are framed differently.

title: The effect of different types of money scarcity on financial services
abstract: In this study, we investigate how financial constraints due to limited money or financial constraints due to social comparisons will affect consumers' preference toward products related to financial advice that are framed differently.

title: SCARcE: The effect of scarcity on fear acquisition, extinction and return of fear
abstract: Common mental disorders, including anxiety disorders, are highly prevalent. However, in contrast to popular belief, anxiety disorders are not a disease of affluence. In all countries around the world, anxiety disorders are more prevalent in people who live in poverty. Nonetheless, psychological research on anxiety disorders and sociological research on poverty have largely been conducted separately, thereby neglecting the potential interaction between the two. As a consequence, the mechanism(s) underlying this relationship between poverty and anxiety disorders are unclear. People living in poverty are constantly faced with a sense of scarcity: they have too few resources to obtain a proper living standard. As a consequence, people in poverty struggle to survive within their environment, and the increased risk for anxiety disorders and accompanying (work) disability further intensifies this struggle. In order to uncover how to break this vicious cycle, we need to uncover which mechanism(s) play a role in the heightened risk of anxiety disorders in those experiencing scarcity. In the current study, we will investigate the impact of experimentally induced scarcity on learning mechanisms that have been shown to play a crucial role in the onset and perpetuation of anxiety disorders, i.e., fear acquisition and -extinction (Beckers et al., 2023).

title: The effect of Money (vs. Time) Scarcity on Financial Services framed in different ways
abstract: In this project, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affect consumers' responses toward financial services and how framing the financial services will affect the responses.

title: Why and for Whom Does Employment Foster Flourishing? A Global Test of Economic Scarcity and Structural Vulnerability
abstract:
Employment is a common and potentially powerful pathway to human flourishing. Using Waves 1–2 of the Global Flourishing Study (≈200,000 adults in 22 countries; ≈ annual lag), we test whether being employed is linked to higher subsequent flourishing, why (economic vs. psychological pathways), and for whom (structural vulnerability).

Guided by Job Demands–Resources (JD-R; Bakker, Demerouti & Sanz-Vergel, 2023) (employment as structure/social/skill resources), Self-Determination Theory (SDT; Deci, Olafsen & Ryan, 2017) (employment can support autonomy/competence), and Conservation of Resources (COR, Hobfoll, Halbesleben, Neveu, & Westman, 2018) (resource gains matter most under strain), and building on VanderWeele’s (2017) flourishing framework, we examine two pathways:

1.	Economic pathway: Income Sufficiency (income adequacy; distinct from worry items in the flourishing index), and
2.	Psychological pathway: Psychological Resources (inner peace, life balance, optimism).

We preregister Structural Economic Vulnerability (SEV) at baseline—a structural index (housing tenure, dependency load, objective income tier)—and test whether SEV amplifies the employment → income-sufficiency path (first-stage moderation). Our primary outcome is the 12-item Secure Flourishing index. We include a discriminant-validity CFA; if Psychological Resources is not empirically distinct from Flourishing, we pre-specify demotion of the psychological pathway to exploratory.


Contribution. We (a) test clean, non-overlapping mechanisms (income sufficiency vs. psychological resources), (b) identify boundary conditions (SEV-conditioned gains) in the active labour force, and (c) deliver policy-relevant evidence on who benefits most from employment’s economic channel.

title: Love Scarcity and Abundance Scale Development Study
abstract: The purpose of this study is to develop a scale of Love Scarcity and Abundance.

title: Love Scarcity and Abundance Study 2
abstract: The purpose of this study is to further evaluate the validity and reliability of the Love Scarcity and Abundance scale.

title: The effect of Money (vs. Time) Scarcity on Financial Services
abstract: In this study, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affects consumers' financial advice-taking and its defensive mechanism.

title: Ambiguity, Scarcity, and Public Service Choice
abstract: We use an online conjoint experiment to test how individuals choose between public service programs when they face outcome uncertainty. We present participants with eight rounds of choices between two programs. We vary uncertainty of three different outcomes and estimate preferences across different levels of perceived scarcity. This plan details the design of the conjoint experiment and the data analysis.

title: Does perceived economic scarcity decrease interpersonal pathogen avoidance? (Study 1)
abstract:
Resource scarcity, especially economic scarcity is a pervasive challenge in contemporary societies. From an evolutionary perspective, humans have evolved a behavioral immune system (BIS) that motivates avoidance of pathogen threats, including avoiding interpersonal contact, especially towards individuals showing signs of illness (Schaller & Park, 2011). However, social interaction also affords critical fitness-relevant benefits (e.g., alliances, status, mating). Thus, individuals face a trade-off: interpersonal pathogen avoidance vs. pursuing interpersonal value via contact.
Based on scarcity theory (Shah et al., 2012), we propose that perceived economic scarcity may shift this trade-off: people who perceive stronger economic scarcity may prioritize interpersonal value goals, which in turn lead them to relax interpersonal pathogen avoidance. Specifically, we proposed a mediating model in the present work: perceived economic scarcity → heightened interpersonal value motivation → reduced interpersonal pathogen avoidance.

title: The effect of Money (vs. Time) Scarcity on Financial Services
abstract: In this study, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affects consumers' emotions and financial advice-taking.

title: SCARcE: The effect of scarcity on fear acquisition and avoidance
abstract: Common mental disorders, including anxiety disorders, are highly prevalent. However, in contrast to popular belief, anxiety disorders are not a disease of affluence. In all countries around the world, anxiety disorders are more prevalent in people who live in poverty. Nonetheless, psychological research on anxiety disorders and sociological research on poverty have largely been conducted separately, thereby neglecting the potential interaction between the two. As a consequence, the mechanism(s) underlying this relationship between poverty and anxiety disorders are unclear. As a consequence, people in poverty struggle to survive within their environment, and the increased risk for anxiety disorders and accompanying (work) disability further intensifies this struggle. title: Resource Scarcity, Outcome Expectations and Emotion Regulation
abstract: This project explores the relationships between scarcity, outcome expectations, emotions and the use of emotion regulation.

title: Money scarcity vs. Time scarcity effect on financial advice-taking and emotions.
abstract: In this study, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affects consumers' emotions and financial advice-taking.

title: Money scarcity vs. Time scarcity Effect on financial advice-taking and moderation of self-affirmation.
abstract: In this study, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affects consumers' emotions and financial advice-taking and how self-affirmation moderates the effects.

title: The effect of Money (vs. Time) Scarcity and the moderation of self-affirmation
abstract: In this study, we investigate how feelings of money (vs. time) scarcity affects consumers' preference towards using financial service and how the self-affirmation moderates the effect.

title: The effect of different types of money scarcity
abstract: In this study, we investigate different types of money scarcity. We compare the effect of money scarcity due to social comparison and money scarcity due to temporal comparison.

title: Perceived economic scarcity and interpersonal objectification - Black-out scenario
abstract:
Theoretical Justification:
In recent decades, socioeconomic inequalities have increased in most countries worldwide (Alvaredo et al., 2018; Wilkinson & Pickett, 2009). An extensive body of research has analyzed their impact on psychological and social well-being, highlighting that these inequalities do not only affect the less affluent but also have consequences for society as a whole (Jetten, 2019; Lynch et al., 2004; Moreno-Bella, Willis, & Moya, 2019; Petkanopoulou et al., 2018; Snowdon, 2010; Wilkinson & Pickett, 2006).
The degree of economic inequality—defined as the gap between the wealthiest and the poorest—serves as a reference point for evaluating one's income and social position. However, it remains unclear how inequality influences the perception of personal wealth and available resources. Recent studies suggest that, because individuals assess their condition by comparing it to others, socioeconomic inequality may affect the perception of one's wealth or, conversely, the scarcity of resources. The same amount of resources may seem sufficient or insufficient depending on the reference group: when compared to a more advantaged group, one might perceive a condition of scarcity, while comparison with a less privileged group could reinforce a sense of abundance (Sánchez-Rodríguez et al., 2019).
If socioeconomic inequalities influence the perception of one’s condition—experienced as either economic security and well-being or, conversely, as scarcity and deprivation—it becomes crucial to understand the effects of this evaluation on both individual and social levels, especially when it leads to the perception of disadvantage.
In this context, the studies conducted by Sendhil Mullainathan and Eldar Shafir (2013) are particularly relevant. They demonstrated that living under severe deprivation—defined by the authors as scarcity, a constant struggle to obtain sufficient resources to meet basic needs—has profound repercussions on cognitive, emotional, and social functioning. Persistent concern for survival leads to a heightened focus on money and the immediate resolution of economic problems (attention tunneling), increasing the cognitive load (Mullainathan & Shafir, 2013; Shah et al., 2018). These mechanisms collectively impair cognitive performance and executive functions (Lichand & Mani, 2020; Shafir, 2017), intensify negative emotions and stress (Li et al., 2023), and hinder the ability to make considered decisions, favoring short-term benefits over long-term outcomes (Caballero et al., 2021; De Bruijn & Antonides, 2022). Moreover, they trigger feelings of social exclusion and ostracism, reinforcing a cycle of vulnerability and isolation (Doolard et al., 2021).
Subsequent studies have shown that the negative cognitive effects of scarcity, known as the scarcity mindset (Cui et al., 2022), do not manifest solely in situations of extreme material deprivation but can emerge across different contexts. The experience of scarcity and the resulting scarcity mindset can affect not only lower socioeconomic classes but also middle and upper classes whenever a resource perceived as essential is deemed insufficient (Piff et al., 2018). For instance, Jiang and colleagues (2024) demonstrated that, regardless of social class, the perception of time scarcity can have the same negative cognitive consequences as economic scarcity. Similarly, Denti and collaborators (2024) found that the perception of insufficient resources in the workplace (materials, time, support) generates a scarcity mindset, while Sharma and colleagues (2023) showed that the same process can arise from facing substantial expenses, such as planning a wedding.
Although most studies have so far focused on the individual-level effects of the scarcity mindset (for a review, see De Almeida et al., 2024), some intriguing evidence suggests that these effects may also extend to social relationships (Civai et al., 2024). Experiencing scarcity appears to reduce empathy toward others (Li et al., 2023) and stimulate competitive behavior (Roux et al., 2015). Furthermore, monetary scarcity may affect moral standards, increasing the tendency to lie for financial gain and to judge moral transgressions aimed at achieving economic benefits less harshly (Sharma et al., 2014). Similarly, time scarcity seems to encourage behaviors focused on achieving personal goals (agentic orientation) while downplaying attention to others and relationship care (communal orientation), fostering the phenomenon of objectifying others (Jiang et al., 2024).
Given the breadth and pervasiveness of these effects, the present study investigates whether experiencing scarcity may similarly influence a fundamental capacity for individual and interpersonal well-being: Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to understand and recognize one’s own and others' cognitive and emotional states (Premack & Woodruff, 1978). This ability underlies all social relationships (Lecce et al., 2017) and is influenced by contextual factors in both its development and expression (Slaughter & de Rosnay, 2017).
Additionally, recent research suggests that the capacity to understand and interpret one's own and others' cognitive and emotional states and the ability to adopt others' perspectives are connected to another psychological process that has garnered increasing scholarly attention: interpersonal objectification (Raguso et al., 2024, in preparation). This process—a specific form of dehumanization where others are perceived and treated as mere objects (Nussbaum, 1995)—has been extensively analyzed in sexual (Pecini et al., 2023) and occupational contexts (Baldissarri et al., 2022), with more recent studies exploring it as a broader phenomenon (Teng et al., 2016; Wang & Krumhuber, 2017).
If living in conditions of scarcity affects Theory of Mind and empathy, as demonstrated for other cognitive and emotional capacities, such an effect may not only have direct consequences on individual and social well-being but could also influence the tendency to perceive others as objects or tools. This would align with existing evidence showing that in conditions of economic and material hardship, attention becomes narrowly focused on acquiring resources necessary to meet basic needs.

Objective:
The aim of the present study is to replicate the previous one, modifying the experimental manipulation. In this case, inspired by the blackout that occurred in Spain on April 28, 2025—which created a widespread temporary scarcity—participants (only individuals residing in Spain) will be divided into three conditions.
In the first condition, representing high scarcity, participants will be asked to imagine themselves in a similar situation to the April 28 blackout, but this time lasting several days, and to imagine they do not have enough money or essential goods at home to get through the blackout.
In the second condition, representing low scarcity, participants will be asked to imagine the same scenario, but this time being well-prepared, with sufficient money and essential goods at home to endure the blackout.
In the third, control condition, participants will be asked to think about their usual daily routine on a typical day in their life.
Through these three conditions, the study aims to investigate whether scarcity may lead to a deterioration in cognitive functioning and an increase in the objectification of others.

title: Racial bias, AP value/scarcity, and placement decisions
abstract:
Racial disparities in higher education can be explained by racial opportunity gaps (Milner, 2012), with highly valuable educational opportunities—such as placement in Advanced Placement (AP) courses—being disproportionately concentrated among White students (Patrick et al., 2020). The opportunity hoarding theory postulates that this inequality is perpetuated by group-based biases and stereotypes created and endorsed by advantaged group members, and that these biases may be manifested in more subtle, implicit (rather than explicit) ways (Lewis & Diamond, 2015). However, this perspective has largely been examined using qualitative approaches thus far, and the psychological processes through which racial bias translates into hoarding behaviors are not well understood. 

The proposed research is a quantitative test of the opportunity hoarding hypothesis within the context of the AP program. We have preliminary evidence that White individuals with greater pro-White/anti-Black implicit bias are more likely to engage in “hoarding” of opportunities to participate in the AP program under circumstances that align with the opportunity hoarding theory (i.e., when opportunities are valuable and scarce; preregistration here [https://osf.io/3v5y4]). In the proposed experiment, we will attempt to replicate these results while also delineating the psychological conditions and mechanisms that link implicit bias with opportunity hoarding behaviors. Specifically, we will adjudicate among three competing hypotheses: Scarcity-Competition (Hypothesis 1a), Value-Feeling Good (Hypothesis 1b), and Combination-Anxiety (Hypothesis 1c). 

We will answer these questions by manipulating the value and scarcity of the opportunity to participate in the AP program and measuring potential mediators.

title: Budgeting and Scarcity Perception
abstract: The objective of this study is to examine whether the behavior of personal, financial budgeting leads people to perceive greater resource scarcity in their environment. Additionally, we look to determine whether this potential increase in scarcity perception leads people to then have a greater sense of perceived stress. We aim to accomplish this by comparing participants’ response time latencies and accuracy to nonwords (e.g., avaagglows, coaxercerce), “neutral” words (e.g., ghost, ocean), “abundance” words (e.g., prosperity, rich), and “scarcity” words (e.g., shortage, barren) through a lexical decision task among a control condition, a financial information condition, and a budgeting condition. Further, we will then compare perceived stress levels among these conditions.

title: A scarcity mindset does not influence economic cheating across or between social classes [STUDY 2]
abstract: Study 2 of the project "A scarcity mindset does not influence economic cheating across or between social classes". This study is a replication of Study 1 with few minor changes.

title: The Effects of Acute and Chronic Financial Scarcity on Economic Cheating
abstract: Pre-registration for the study "The Effects of Acute and Chronic Financial Scarcity on Economic Cheating".

title: Immorality at the end of the tunnel: The effect of scarcity-induced focus regulation on ethical blind spots
abstract: Most of us perceive ourselves as moral people, yet we do also occasionally fall prey to the temptation of engaging in lucrative but immoral practices. In the quest of escaping the moral dissonance that often follows, we resort to ever more creative ways of justifying our immoral behavior. Recent research suggests that ethical blind spots in individuals’ eye movements could serve as a remedy to resolve this internal quandary and thus have a facilitative effect on immorality: “if we have not seen the immoral information, we cannot act upon it”. While various personality traits are suggested to affect individuals’ inclination to engage in such self-deceptive behavior, research on how contextual factors such as relative resource scarcity influence this relationship is lacking. In this experiment we test, for the first time, how relative resource scarcity is manifested in gaze patterns and specifically whether such gaze patterns are predictive of unethical conduct. Our research aims to outline theoretical and practical implications of how contextual factors influence the use of self-serving justifications, in turn influencing the magnitude of unethical conduct.

title: Money Scarcity vs. Time Scarcity Effect on social exclusion and premium products
abstract: In this study, we investigate how the scarcity effects can differ depending on the source of scarcity. We compare time scarcity due to social comparison, money scarcity due to socal comparison, and money scarcity due to temporal comparison. We investigate how the scarcity effects differ on the feelings of social exclusion and product preferences.

title: Scarcity & Perceptions of Inequality (EU)
abstract:
Experiences of financial scarcity (i.e., perceptions of “not having enough” financial resources) are prevalent across the modern world (Elbæk et al., 2023) and can substantially influence human judgement and decision-making (Shah et al., 2012) – often for the worse (Mani et al., 2013). Higher economic inequality has been shown to intensity the financial hardship of people living with experiences of scarcity (Jachimowicz et al., 2020), yet no prior work has investigated specifically how experiences of scarcity might influence people’s subjective perceptions of economic inequality; subjective perceptions which, compared to more objective indicators, are often stronger predictors of policy attitudes, public health and societal well-being (Jachimowicz et al., 2022). Using two multinational studies (Total N = 12,777) spanning 41, and 30 countries around the world, respectively, we have found initial evidence suggesting that experiences of financial scarcity is related to perceiving greater inequality in society. That is, those who perceive themselves as struggling financially relative to others also believe their society is characterized by greater inequality. Analyses further demonstrated that this relationship may depend on how economically mobile a person believes their society to be. However, this relationship is correlational and experimental evidence is needed to determine causality. Therefore, the current project aims to focus on uncovering the causal relationship between experiences of financial scarcity and how this might alter perceptions of the overall economic distribution. We will additionally explore the role of perceived economic mobility in qualifying this relationship. 

References: 
Elbæk, C. T., Mitkidis, P., Aarøe, L., & Otterbring, T. (2023). Subjective socioeconomic status and income inequality are associated with self-reported morality across 67 countries. Nature Communications, 14(1), 5453. 
Jachimowicz, J. M., Szaszi, B., Lukas, M., Smerdon, D., Prabhu, J., & Weber, E. U. (2020). Higher economic inequality intensifies the financial hardship of people living in poverty by fraying the community buffer. Nature Human Behaviour, 4(7), 702-712. 
Jachimowicz, J. M., Davidai, S., Goya-Tocchetto, D., Szaszi, B., Day, M. V., Tepper, S. J., Phillips, L. T., Mirza, M. U., Ordabayeva, N., & Hauser, O. P. (2022). Inequality in researchers’ minds: Four guiding questions for studying subjective perceptions of economic inequality. Journal of Economic Surveys. 37, 1534-1561.
Mani, A., Mullainathan, S., Shafir, E., & Zhao, J. (2013). Poverty impedes cognitive function. Science, 341(6149), 976-980.
Shah, A. K., Mullainathan, S., & Shafir, E. (2012). Some consequences of having too little. Science, 338(6107), 682-685.

title: The mediation effect of cognitive load on the relationship between scarcity and perspective-taking_Study 3.
abstract: This is Study 3 of the following experiments: Study 1 - https://osf.io/wkehv/?view_only=7f8183bb434941ae926f37d9e7668715 Study 2 - https://osf.io/pdsbt/?view_only=416ee4a471894b7295598c580f217afa
